summary: A merchant vessel in the southbound corridor reported a nearby uncrewed aerial system detonation; no damage or casualties were reported.
Multiple vessels reported simultaneous loss of satellite navigation fix over several hours; positions recovered west of the traffic separation scheme.
Unarmed perpetrators boarded a bulk carrier underway at night and left with engine spares before the crew mustered; no injuries reported.
A surface drone struck the hull of a merchant ship in the strait approaches, causing a fire that the crew contained; the vessel proceeded under her own power.
A product tanker lost communication south of the delta; contact was re-established four days later with cargo partially transferred and crew unharmed.
Sustained interference produced onshore AIS tracks for ships at anchor across the eastern anchorages, consistent with area jamming rather than individual spoofing.
A skiff approached a slow-steaming bulk carrier and two perpetrators reached the poop deck before the embarked security team fired warning shots; the skiff withdrew.
A drifting contact mine was sighted and neutralised by naval forces on the western grain corridor; traffic resumed within hours.
Two aerial drones were intercepted near a northbound convoy; debris fell within one nautical mile of a container ship without causing damage.
Regional forces diverted and detained a tanker following a flag-state dispute; the vessel was released after nineteen days at anchor.
A cluster of impossible-speed AIS tracks formed a circular pattern around the eastern approaches, a signature consistent with systematic interference.
Armed perpetrators boarded an anchored offshore supply vessel and took valuables; local naval response arrived after they had departed.
A merchant ship reported an explosion off her port quarter attributed to an intercepted aerial system; transit continued with a naval escort joining.
Port-bound vessels reported repeated navigation-system resets; AIS positions intermittently displaced up to forty nautical miles inland.
A dhow was taken by an armed group and used as a mothership for ten days before being abandoned; the crew was recovered safely.
A tanker in ballast reported a near-miss from a surface drone that detonated approximately 500 metres astern; no damage sustained.
Three perpetrators boarded a tanker in the eastbound lane and escaped with stores when discovered by the duty watch; the fourth incident in the strait this year.
Authorities detained a small products tanker alleged to be carrying unmanifested fuel; the case was referred to local courts.
Interference intensified over a seventy-two-hour window with dozens of vessels reporting degraded positioning; effects extended into the western Gulf of Oman.
An escorted convoy reported two intercepted aerial systems during a night transit; no vessel was struck and the convoy maintained schedule.
A fishing vessel was boarded and her catch and equipment taken; the incident follows a seasonal pattern in the area's artisanal fleet.
A moored mine was located by survey operations near the coastal route and destroyed in place; the corridor reopened the same day.
A container vessel reported splinter damage to deck containers from an intercepted drone; no casualties, transit completed to schedule.
Approach-channel traffic reported synchronized position jumps; harbour control shifted to radar-based sequencing for the evening.
An attempted takeover of a low-freeboard bulker was abandoned when the crew retreated to the citadel and a coalition warship closed the position.
A new interference cluster displaced AIS tracks onto the Musandam peninsula; the pattern persisted intermittently through the week.
Perpetrators boarded a drifting tanker awaiting orders and left with personal effects after a two-hour search; crew unharmed in the citadel.
A southbound tanker reported an explosion close aboard attributed to an uncrewed surface vessel; minor superficial damage, no injuries.
